metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the baked-SHA architectural decision (triple-registration contract, silent-skip risk, resolver fallback order) in an ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or inline design note so future maintainers can maintain all three coordination points together.
findings.finding: The novel build-time SHA baking pattern — specifically the non-obvious triple-registration requirement (custom hook + artifacts glob + .gitignore) and the resolver fallback contract — is not captured in any ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update. Future maintainers or contributors could silently break the SHA-shipping pipeline by touching any one of three coordination points without understanding the coupling.
findings.location: ## What / AC line 2 / 'Register it in pyproject.toml'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/adr/0NNN-baked-build-sha.md` (or a `CLAUDE.md` section 'Build-time SHA baking') that documents: (1) the triple-registration requirement and why each part is needed, (2) the silent-skip failure mode, (3) the resolver priority chain, and (4) a pointer to AC5's wheel-contents test as the canary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the story-1 resolver wraps `import _build_info` in a `try/except ImportError` (or equivalent guard) so a missing generated file falls through to the omit-last path, not a crash — and add this case to the AC.
findings.finding: The resolver's consumer behavior when `_build_info.py` does not exist at all (fresh clone, no build step run yet) is unspecified. The plan only specifies tolerance for `COMMIT = None` (file generated but git absent), not for an `ImportError` when the module is missing entirely — leaving a gap in the consumer's tolerance of the state's absence.
findings.location: What — 'Extend the story-1 resolver fallback' / AC line: 'Resolver falls back to the baked SHA on a non-git install'
findings.suggested_fix: In the resolver fallback extension, add: `try: from rebar._build_info import COMMIT as _baked_sha; except ImportError: _baked_sha = None` so the omit-last path is reached when the file has never been generated. Add a test case for this scenario to AC bullet 5.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_build_info.py` to an `artifacts` entry under `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]` (or confirm that sdist consumers are expected to re-run the hook from a fresh git tree, and document that decision).
findings.finding: The plan adds `src/rebar/_build_info.py` to the wheel `artifacts` glob but does not address the sdist. The sdist uses an `include = ["src/rebar", ...]` allowlist, but hatchling still applies gitignore filtering to included paths — a git-ignored `_build_info.py` under `src/rebar` would be silently dropped from the sdist just as it would from the wheel without the `artifacts` entry.
findings.location: What section: 'Add src/rebar/_build_info.py ... to the artifacts glob under [tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]'; pyproject.toml [tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]
findings.suggested_fix: Add `artifacts = ["src/rebar/_build_info.py"]` under `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]` in `pyproject.toml`, mirroring the wheel treatment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/unit/test_engine_dir.py::test_wheel_contains_no_compiled_bytecode` as a consumer of the new build hook, and confirm whether the hook's `src/rebar/_build_info.py` write-side-effect during in-process wheel builds needs cleanup or isolation in that test.
findings.finding: The existing `tests/unit/test_engine_dir.py::test_wheel_contains_no_compiled_bytecode` builds the wheel in-process via `hatchling.builders.wheel.WheelBuilder`. Once `hatch_build.py` is registered, this test will invoke the new build hook (including the `git rev-parse` subprocess) on every run, but the plan does not enumerate this test as a consumer or address potential failures when the test runs in a CI environment where git is available but the hook's output changes the wheel contents.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Tests' subsection to 'What' (or 'Scope') describing where test files live, how the baked-fallback unit tests will mock `_build_info`, and — critically — how the wheel-integration test (requires invoking the build system) will be structured and executed in CI.
findings.finding: AC #5 specifies three non-trivial test scenarios — including a wheel-build integration test ('a built wheel actually contains `_build_info.py` with a SHA') — but the 'What' and 'Scope' sections contain no corresponding description of test deliverables, test file locations, or how the wheel-integration test will be executed (e.g., requires invoking hatchling in CI or a subprocess). The test work is an orphan criterion with no backing plan body.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #5 vs. What / Scope sections

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `hatch_build.py` that ALL subprocess/git failures (non-zero exit, OSError, unexpected output) are caught and emit a visible build warning before falling back to `COMMIT = None`, rather than silently degrading.
findings.finding: `hatch_build.py` is specified to write `COMMIT = None` when 'built outside a git tree', but the plan does not specify how other `git rev-parse` subprocess failures (e.g., shallow/partial clone where HEAD exists but the command errors, `git` not on PATH in a restricted build env that still has a `.git` directory, or an OSError from the subprocess itself) are handled — these could silently emit `COMMIT = None` without any build-time warning to the packager.
findings.location: hatch_build.py / AC line 1 ('a non-git build writes COMMIT = None (graceful)')
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `subprocess.run(['git', 'rev-parse', '--short', 'HEAD'], ...)` call in a broad try/except (catching `subprocess.CalledProcessError`, `OSError`, and empty output), log a `warnings.warn` or `print` to stderr on any failure path before writing `COMMIT = None`, so packagers see a notice in build output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `_build_info.py` also needs to be listed in an `artifacts` stanza under `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]` (or confirm hatchling's sdist `include` allowlist supersedes `.gitignore` filtering for that path).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that adding `_build_info.py` to the wheel `artifacts` glob is sufficient to ensure it ships in the wheel. However, the `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]` stanza uses an `include = ["src/rebar", ...]` allowlist, not a git-tracked-files filter — but hatchling's sdist builder still excludes git-ignored files that aren't in an `artifacts` list. The plan only specifies adding `_build_info.py` to the wheel artifacts glob and says nothing about sdist inclusion, leaving the baked SHA absent from source distributions.
findings.location: ## What — 'Add `src/rebar/_build_info.py` to `.gitignore` AND to the `artifacts` glob under `[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `artifacts = ["src/rebar/_build_info.py"]` under `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]` in `pyproject.toml`, mirroring the wheel stanza.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC #1 to specify that ANY non-zero return code or empty output from `git rev-parse` (not just absence of git) also writes `COMMIT = None`, matching the 'graceful omit' contract.
findings.finding: The build hook's error-handling protocol for a partial git failure (git is present but `rev-parse --short HEAD` returns non-zero, e.g. a detached HEAD with no commits, a shallow clone with no history, or a corrupted repo) is unspecified. The plan names only the 'non-git build' (no git binary) case as graceful.
findings.location: AC line 1: 'a non-git build writes COMMIT = None (graceful)'
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC #1 to: 'a non-git build OR any git failure (non-zero exit, empty output) writes `COMMIT = None` (graceful omit).' Add to What: 'The hook treats any OSError, non-zero git exit, or empty SHA as equivalent to a non-git environment and writes `COMMIT = None`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an end-to-end AC (or extend AC5) that installs the built wheel into a temporary non-git virtualenv and asserts the resolver returns the baked short SHA, not None, to exercise the full cutover path as it runs in production.
findings.finding: The resolver's baked-SHA fallback path as it will run in a real non-git PyPI/wheel install is not exercised end-to-end: AC5 covers the two halves (wheel contains the file; unit test with git absent) but no AC requires installing the built wheel in a non-git environment and confirming the resolver returns the baked SHA — the new cutover code path is therefore only validated by component tests that can be satisfied without the full chain ever executing.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5; What section (resolver fallback)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the 'What' or 'Why' section explaining why a generated `_build_info.py` module is the chosen vehicle (e.g., simpler import path, testable in isolation, well-precedented pattern, avoids custom-metadata fragility across package managers).
findings.finding: The plan states WHY baking is needed (PyPI installs lack a git checkout) but provides no positive rationale for WHY the chosen vehicle — a generated `_build_info.py` module — is the right mechanism over structurally-different alternatives such as storing the SHA as a custom distribution metadata key via a hatchling metadata hook. The rubric requires a positive rationale for the chosen approach, not just a rationale for the problem being solved.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the 'What' bullet on `hatch_build.py`: '(A generated module is preferred over a custom distribution-metadata key because `importlib.metadata` custom-field access is not uniformly supported across all package managers, and a plain module attribute import is simpler to consume and to test in isolation.)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC5's wheel-contains check (e.g., `python -m build --wheel && unzip -l dist/*.whl | grep _build_info` or a pytest fixture using `zipfile`) so the criterion is mechanically verifiable.
findings.finding: AC5's 'a built wheel actually contains _build_info.py with a SHA' claim has no concrete proving command described, leaving the verification method undefined (e.g., no mention of `unzip -l dist/*.whl | grep _build_info` or a pytest fixture that builds and inspects the wheel artifact).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5: 'Proven by: `python -m build --wheel && python -c "import zipfile, glob; assert any(\'_build_info\'in n for n in zipfile.ZipFile(glob.glob(\'dist/*.whl\')[0]).namelist())"`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm story 1's SHA resolver (live-git-first, short-SHA, with the three-step fallback chain) exists or will land before this story begins; if not, this plan must either define the full resolver or be re-sequenced.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Depends on story 1's resolver' and 'Extend the story-1 resolver fallback to live-git-first, build-baked-second, omit-last', implying a SHA resolver module/function with this fallback chain already exists. A full codebase search finds no such resolver: `signing.py:head_sha()` returns the full HEAD SHA (not short) for a single live-git path only, with no baked fallback. No module implementing the three-step chain (live → baked → omit) exists anywhere in `src/rebar/`.
findings.location: ## Scope / ## What — 'Extend the story-1 resolver fallback'; 'Depends on story 1's resolver'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific file/function (from story 1's resolver) that will be modified to add the `live-git-first → build-baked-second → omit-last` fallback, so AC #3 has a concrete, locatable edit target.
findings.finding: The plan says 'Extend the story-1 resolver fallback' but names no specific file path for where the fallback wiring lands. No existing 'version/SHA resolver' module was found in `src/rebar`; it is entirely the product of story 1 (a prerequisite). Without a named file, implementers cannot determine which module to edit.
findings.location: What section: 'Extend the story-1 resolver fallback to live-git-first, build-baked-second, omit-last'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why a custom `BuildHookInterface` is chosen over `setuptools-scm`'s `write_to`/`version_file` feature (which bakes a commit SHA into a generated Python file at build time with graceful fallback, without requiring a dynamic version scheme), or confirm that hatch-vcs/setuptools-scm cannot meet this need with `version = "0.6.0"` kept static.
findings.finding: The plan dismisses hatch-vcs with 'do NOT adopt hatch-vcs' but conflates two distinct capabilities: hatch-vcs / setuptools-scm can bake a commit SHA into a generated `_version.py`-style file WITHOUT changing the version scheme, using `write_to` / `version_file` with a static fallback version. The plan introduces a custom `BuildHookInterface` that replicates exactly what setuptools-scm's `write_to` feature does (generate a Python file with a SHA at build time, graceful fallback when no git). This is the established prior art for the stated goal, and the plan should explicitly justify why a custom hook is preferred over the existing tooling.
findings.location: What section / 'Keep package version STATIC (`pyproject.toml` `version`); do NOT adopt hatch-vcs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `rebar-version:` manifest line prefix string (the schema key, e.g. `_REBAR_VERSION_PREFIX = "rebar-version:"`) and the exact format `rebar-version: <version> (<sha>[-dirty>])` in this plan's What section or an accompanying constant spec, so story 1 and the parser can share the same literal.
findings.finding: The `rebar-version:` manifest line prefix/format is defined only in the parent epic (jira-reb-596), not in this story plan. The story's AC and What sections never name the key string, line format, or where in the manifest line ordering it is inserted — leaving implementers without a schema anchor for the new manifest field this story's build hook enables.
findings.location: What section / AC line 'Resolver falls back to the baked SHA on a non-git install'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'The resolver helper writes a `rebar-version:` manifest line with format `rebar-version: {version} ({sha}[-dirty])` (e.g. `rebar-version: 0.6.0 (gc1a2b3c4-dirty)`); omit the parenthetical when the SHA is absent. Store the prefix as `_REBAR_VERSION_PREFIX = "rebar-version:"` in the same module as `_REGVER_PREFIX`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `manifest_rebar_version()` parser implementation to this story's scope (it is a small, standalone function mirroring `manifest_regver()`), or explicitly name the sibling story that owns it so the gap is tracked.
findings.finding: The `manifest_rebar_version()` parser — required by the parent epic (AC #4: 'A manifest_rebar_version() parser (mirroring manifest_regver()) extracts the stamp; returns None for pre-stamp manifests') and needed for the `show` / `verify_signature` surfacing called out in epic AC #6 — is absent from this story's scope without an explicit deferral to a named sibling story.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Build-time SHA capture + fallback wiring only.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
